name: test-run-user-scenarios
description: Test-category scenario validation skill. Simulate realistic users and agents running the framework through happy, failure, and weird cases, then report where the skill surface is confusing, brittle, or missing guardrails.
purpose: Simulate real user and agent usage with concrete inputs and expected outputs, then expose where the framework breaks, confuses, or under-specifies behavior.
when_to_use: Need to validate the framework against realistic use cases instead of only happy-path examples.
Need concrete user and agent scenarios with varied inputs, expected outputs, and observable pass/fail signals.
Need to include success cases, failure cases, and weird-but-plausible misuse cases before release or major refactors.
do_not_use: Need unit or integration test code written directly.
Need only narrow test-gap analysis for one code path.
Need final contract verification after the work is already complete.

required_inputs.name: SYSTEM_UNDER_TEST
required_inputs.description: Which framework surface is being exercised.

required_inputs.name: PRIMARY_USERS
required_inputs.description: Realistic user or agent personas and the job each one is trying to finish.

required_inputs.name: SCENARIO_COUNT
required_inputs.description: Target number of scenarios to generate and run. Default to 6 when omitted.

required_inputs.name: FAILURE_CLASSES
required_inputs.description: Failure or misuse classes to force into the scenario set.

required_inputs.name: KNOWN_CONSTRAINTS
required_inputs.description: Scope, release, or realism constraints for the scenario run.

input_contract_notes: PRIMARY_USERS should be grounded in plausible roles and goals, not fictional filler personas with no concrete job.
SCENARIO_COUNT should stay small enough that each scenario can actually be exercised and observed.
FAILURE_CLASSES should force meaningful breakage or misuse paths, not random nonsense with no learning value.

structured_outputs.name: USE_CASE_MATRIX
structured_outputs.description: Concrete happy-path use cases with explicit inputs and expected signals.

structured_outputs.name: FAILURE_CASES
structured_outputs.description: Realistic failure or misuse scenarios that should reveal brittle behavior or bad affordances.

structured_outputs.name: ODD_CASES
structured_outputs.description: Weird but plausible scenarios worth exercising.

structured_outputs.name: EXECUTION_LOG
structured_outputs.description: What was actually exercised and what happened.

structured_outputs.name: USABILITY_FINDINGS
structured_outputs.description: Framework surface problems found through scenario execution.

structured_outputs.name: NEXT_FIXES
structured_outputs.description: Smallest fixes or follow-up checks that improve real-world usability.

output_contract_notes: USE_CASE_MATRIX should use concrete prompts, macros, or inputs that a real caller could plausibly produce.
EXECUTION_LOG must separate observed results from expected results; do not backfill success without an actual run or observable signal.
FAILURE_CASES and ODD_CASES should exist even when the happy path passes.
neutrality_rules: Do not invent passing outcomes for scenarios that were not actually exercised.
Prefer plausible misuse and ambiguity over theatrical nonsense.
Separate framework confusion, skill-contract ambiguity, and parser/runtime failure.
execution_constraints: Act as both caller and agent when constructing and exercising scenarios.
Include at least one happy-path, one failure-path, and one weird-but-plausible case in every run.
Prefer realistic prompts and inputs that a maintainer, contributor, or first-time user would actually type.
default_program:
[stages: preflight>detect>analyze>review>handoff>audit |
 scope: repo|paths(glob,...) |
 policy: evidence,deterministic-output |
 lens: kahneman-tversky |
 output: md(contract=v1)]
lens_rationale: Scenario testing should resist idealized happy-path bias and instead check what real callers are actually likely to do.
example_invocation: $compose + $test-run-user-scenarios + @skills + [Simulate a first-time maintainer, a rushed releaser, and a confused docs contributor choosing skills for real work. Include happy, failure, and weird cases.]
